intent: Check whether the candidate understands a TypeORM Entity as the persistence mapping between a TypeScript class and a database table, including decorators, columns, primary keys, and schema implications.
primaryQuestion: What is an Entity in TypeORM, and how do you define one?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that an Entity is a class decorated with @Entity that TypeORM maps to a database table.

requirements.description: Defines an entity as a TypeScript class used by TypeORM for persistence
Mentions the @Entity decorator on the class
Explains that TypeORM maps the entity to a database table

expectedSignals.label: Mentions defining properties with decorators such as @PrimaryGeneratedColumn and @Column.

requirements.description: Uses @PrimaryGeneratedColumn or equivalent to define the primary key
Uses @Column for persisted scalar properties
Uses @CreateDateColumn or @UpdateDateColumn where timestamp auditing is part of the example

expectedSignals.label: Understands that TypeScript types alone do not fully define database schema details such as length, nullability, uniqueness, enum, precision, or column type.

requirements.description: Mentions database column options such as length, nullable, unique, default, enum, precision, or type
Distinguishes TypeScript property types from actual database column definitions
Sets important database constraints explicitly instead of assuming TypeScript annotations enforce them

expectedSignals.label: Shows a small example such as UserEntity with id, email, name, createdAt, and updatedAt.

requirements.description: Includes an id primary key in the example entity
Includes persisted user fields such as email and displayName or name
Includes createdAt and updatedAt timestamp columns when sketching the entity

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that entities represent persistence structure and should not automatically become public API response contracts.

requirements.description: Separates persistence entity structure from public request or response DTO contracts
Avoids exposing persistence-only or sensitive fields directly through API responses
Keeps request-only fields out of the entity when they are not persisted

scoringHints.description: Defines entity mapping clearly, gives a concrete decorated class example, and distinguishes persistence metadata from TypeScript-only typing and API DTOs.
Understands @Entity and column decorators but misses some schema options or boundary discussion.
Can repeat decorator names but cannot connect them to table/column mapping.
Answer does not show practical TypeORM entity knowledge.

followUps.question: Can you sketch a User entity with id, email, displayName, createdAt, and updatedAt?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete decorator and column-mapping knowledge.

followUps.question: Which fields would you keep out of the entity even if they appear in an API request?
followUps.purpose: Check separation between persistence model and request contract.

en.title: TypeORM entity basics
en.displayQuestion: What is an Entity in TypeORM, and how do you define one?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands class-to-table mapping with decorators.
guidance: Ask for a concrete entity class example.
Listen for @Entity, @Column, primary key, schema options, and DTO boundary awareness.
Push beyond memorizing decorator names into database mapping behavior.
commonMistakes: Treating entities as TypeScript interfaces.
Assuming all class fields are persisted automatically.
Using entities as public API DTOs without thinking about exposure.

vi.title: Nền tảng Entity trong TypeORM
vi.displayQuestion: Entity trong TypeORM là gì, và bạn định nghĩa một Entity như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về mapping từ class sang table bằng decorator.
guidance: Yêu cầu ví dụ class entity cụ thể.
Tìm các ý về @Entity, @Column, primary key, schema option và ranh giới với DTO.
Đào sâu hành vi mapping database, không chỉ nhớ tên decorator.
commonMistakes: Xem entity như TypeScript interface.
Cho rằng mọi field trong class đều tự được persist.
Dùng entity làm DTO public API mà không xét rủi ro lộ dữ liệu.

ja.title: TypeORM Entity の基礎
ja.displayQuestion: TypeORM の Entity とは何で、どのように定義しますか。
ja.displayIntent: decorator による class-to-table mapping の理解を確認する。
guidance: 具体的な entity class の例を求める。
@Entity、@Column、primary key、schema option、DTO との境界を確認する。
decorator 名の暗記ではなく database mapping の動作まで掘り下げる。
commonMistakes: entity を TypeScript interface として扱う。
class field がすべて自動で永続化されると思い込む。
公開 API の DTO として entity をそのまま使い、データ露出を考えない。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Entities
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Entity, @Column, primary generated columns, and special date columns.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands the Repository pattern in TypeORM and the NestJS wiring required to inject entity-specific repositories into services.
primaryQuestion: What is the Repository pattern, and how do you inject a Repository into a Service?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that a Repository encapsulates data access operations for one entity type.

requirements.description: Defines a repository as data access for one entity type
Explains that repository methods encapsulate database persistence operations
Distinguishes repository data access from service-level business workflow

expectedSignals.label: Mentions Repository<User> or similar as the main API for find, create, save, update, delete, and query builder access.

requirements.description: Mentions Repository<User> or an equivalent typed repository
Names common methods such as find, create, save, update, delete, or remove
Mentions query builder access for queries that exceed simple repository methods

expectedSignals.label: In NestJS, registers the entity with TypeOrmModule.forFeature([User]) in the owning module.

requirements.description: Imports TypeOrmModule.forFeature([User]) in the module that owns the service
Explains that forFeature registers the repository provider in that module context
Checks the owning module imports first when Nest cannot resolve UserRepository

expectedSignals.label: Injects the repository using @InjectRepository(User) in the service constructor.

requirements.description: Uses @InjectRepository(User) for constructor injection
Types the constructor parameter as Repository<User> or equivalent
Injects and uses the repository from a service or data-access layer rather than directly in the controller

expectedSignals.label: Understands that repository methods should be used from services or data-access layers, not scattered through controllers.

requirements.description: Keeps controllers focused on transport concerns and delegates business logic to services
Places repository calls and business rules in service or data-access code
Connects the boundary to testability and maintainable dependency injection

scoringHints.description: Connects repository responsibility, Nest module registration, @InjectRepository wiring, service-layer usage, and testability.
Knows the injection syntax but gives limited reasoning about module ownership or responsibility boundaries.
Can name Repository but cannot wire it correctly in NestJS.
Answer does not demonstrate usable repository or DI knowledge.

followUps.question: If Nest says it cannot resolve UserRepository in UsersService, what file would you check first and why?
followUps.purpose: Verify practical NestJS repository provider wiring.

followUps.question: What belongs in the service versus directly inside the repository call?
followUps.purpose: Check responsibility boundaries.

en.title: Repository pattern and DI
en.displayQuestion: What is the Repository pattern, and how do you inject a Repository into a Service?
en.displayIntent: Check TypeORM repository responsibility and NestJS dependency injection wiring.
guidance: Ask for TypeOrmModule.forFeature and @InjectRepository details.
Look for service-layer ownership rather than repository calls from controllers.
Probe what happens when the provider is not registered.
commonMistakes: Forgetting forFeature.
Manually constructing repositories.
Putting database calls directly in controllers.

vi.title: Repository pattern và DI
vi.displayQuestion: Repository pattern là gì, và bạn inject Repository vào Service như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra trách nhiệm của TypeORM Repository và cách wiring dependency injection trong NestJS.
guidance: Hỏi rõ TypeOrmModule.forFeature và @InjectRepository.
Tìm tín hiệu service sở hữu business flow thay vì controller gọi thẳng database.
Đào sâu lỗi xảy ra khi provider chưa được đăng ký.
commonMistakes: Quên forFeature.
Tự new repository.
Đưa database call trực tiếp vào controller.

ja.title: Repository pattern と DI
ja.displayQuestion: Repository pattern とは何で、Service に Repository をどのように inject しますか。
ja.displayIntent: TypeORM Repository の責務と NestJS dependency injection wiring を確認する。
guidance: TypeOrmModule.forFeature と @InjectRepository の説明を求める。
controller から直接 database を呼ぶのではなく service-layer ownership を確認する。
provider が登録されていない場合の失敗を掘り下げる。
commonMistakes: forFeature を忘れる。
repository を手動で new する。
database call を controller に直接置く。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Repository
sourceReferences.note: Used for repository purpose and common operations.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Database
sourceReferences.note: Used for TypeOrmModule.forFeature and @InjectRepository integration.

intent: Check whether the candidate can choose the correct TypeORM read method and understands return shapes, filters, relation loading, and not-found behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between find(), findOne(), findOneBy(), and findOneOrFail()?

expectedSignals.label: States that find returns an array and supports find options such as where, relations, order, skip, and take.

requirements.description: States that find returns an array of entities
Mentions find options such as where and relations
Mentions order, skip, take, or other list-oriented options

expectedSignals.label: Explains that findOne returns one entity or null using richer find options.

requirements.description: Explains that findOne returns one entity or null
Explains that findOne can use richer find options such as where plus relations or ordering
Chooses findOne over findOneBy when relations or richer options are required

expectedSignals.label: Explains that findOneBy returns one entity or null using a simpler where condition object.

requirements.description: Explains that findOneBy returns one entity or null
Explains that findOneBy uses a simple where condition object
Chooses findOneBy for simple primary-key or field lookups when no richer options are needed

expectedSignals.label: Explains that findOneOrFail throws if no row is found and should be handled intentionally.

requirements.description: Explains that findOneOrFail throws when no matching entity exists
Mentions converting not-found behavior into an intentional domain or HTTP response
Avoids letting repository not-found exceptions leak unintentionally

expectedSignals.label: Mentions choosing by desired cardinality, options needed, and not-found behavior instead of using one method everywhere.

requirements.description: Chooses method based on whether the caller expects many rows or one row
Chooses method based on whether richer relations/order options are needed
Chooses method based on whether missing rows should return null or throw

scoringHints.description: Clearly compares cardinality, options shape, null versus thrown error, relation loading, and service-level error mapping.
Understands most method differences but misses relation options or error-handling implications.
Knows method names but chooses them by habit rather than behavior.
Answer is too vague to verify practical TypeORM querying knowledge.

followUps.question: Which method would you use for GET /users/:id, and what exactly happens if the user does not exist?
followUps.purpose: Connect method choice to API behavior.

followUps.question: When would findOne be more appropriate than findOneBy?
followUps.purpose: Check awareness of richer find options.

en.title: TypeORM find method differences
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between find(), findOne(), findOneBy(), and findOneOrFail()?
en.displayIntent: Check read-method selection, return values, and not-found handling.
guidance: Compare array versus single result.
Discuss null versus thrown error.
Ask about relation loading and richer find options.
commonMistakes: Using find() then taking index 0 for a unique lookup.
Letting findOneOrFail leak an unhandled error.
Ignoring relation and ordering requirements.

vi.title: Khác nhau giữa các hàm find
vi.displayQuestion: find(), findOne(), findOneBy() và findOneOrFail() khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cách chọn read method, kiểu dữ liệu trả về và xử lý not-found.
guidance: So sánh array với single result.
Nói rõ null khác thrown error thế nào.
Hỏi thêm về load relation và find option phức tạp hơn.
commonMistakes: Dùng find() rồi lấy phần tử đầu cho lookup unique.
Để findOneOrFail ném lỗi không được map sang response phù hợp.
Bỏ qua nhu cầu relation và ordering.

ja.title: find 系メソッドの違い
ja.displayIntent: read method の選択、戻り値、not-found handling を確認する。
guidance: array と single result を比較する。
null と thrown error の違いを説明させる。
relation loading と richer find options について確認する。
commonMistakes: unique lookup で find() して先頭だけ取る。
findOneOrFail の例外を適切な response に変換しない。
relation や ordering の必要性を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Repository
sourceReferences.note: Used for find, findOne, findOneBy, and findOneOrFail repository methods.

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Find Options
sourceReferences.note: Used for find options, relations, ordering, pagination, and filtering.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands the practical difference between creating entity instances, persisting with entity lifecycle behavior, and executing direct insert operations.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between save(), insert(), and create() in TypeORM?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that create builds an entity instance from a plain object but does not write to the database.

requirements.description: Explains that repository.create builds an entity instance from a plain object
States that create does not write or commit anything to the database
Distinguishes object construction from persistence

expectedSignals.label: Explains that save persists an entity and can insert or update depending on whether the primary key exists.

requirements.description: Explains that save writes an entity to the database
Explains that save may insert or update depending on primary key or entity state
Uses save when working with entity instances or persistence semantics that need entity behavior

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that save returns an entity and may involve lifecycle hooks, cascades, and extra existence checks depending on usage.

requirements.description: Mentions that save returns the persisted entity
Mentions lifecycle hooks, cascades, subscribers, or relation graph behavior as save-related considerations
Mentions possible extra existence checks or overhead compared with direct insert

expectedSignals.label: Explains that insert performs a direct insert operation and does not behave like full entity save.

requirements.description: Explains that insert performs a direct database insert operation
Explains that insert does not behave like a full entity save with the same lifecycle semantics
Chooses insert for simple direct or bulk insertion where entity graph behavior is not required

expectedSignals.label: Chooses insert for simple bulk/direct insertion when entity lifecycle behavior is not needed, and save when working with entity graphs or upsert-like persistence semantics.

requirements.description: Chooses insert for importing many simple rows when performance and direct SQL shape matter
Chooses save when cascades, entity hooks, or entity graph persistence are intentional
States the trade-off between speed/directness and entity lifecycle behavior

scoringHints.description: Accurately separates in-memory construction, entity persistence semantics, direct insert behavior, lifecycle implications, and performance trade-offs.
Knows the basic difference but misses lifecycle or performance details.
Confuses object creation with persistence or treats save and insert as identical.
Answer does not show usable TypeORM write-method knowledge.

followUps.question: For importing 10,000 simple rows, would you use save or insert? What trade-off are you accepting?
followUps.purpose: Check performance and lifecycle reasoning.

followUps.question: If repository.create(dto) succeeds, has anything been committed to the database yet?
followUps.purpose: Catch confusion between instance creation and persistence.

en.title: save, insert, and create
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between save(), insert(), and create() in TypeORM?
en.displayIntent: Check write-method semantics, persistence behavior, and performance trade-offs.
guidance: Separate in-memory entity creation from database writes.
Discuss insert versus save behavior and lifecycle implications.
Use a bulk-import or create-user example.
commonMistakes: Thinking create() inserts a row.
Using save() for every bulk operation.
Expecting insert() to behave exactly like save().

vi.title: save, insert và create
vi.displayQuestion: save(), insert() và create() trong TypeORM khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra semantics của write method, hành vi persist và trade-off performance.
guidance: Tách việc tạo entity trong memory khỏi việc ghi database.
Nói về insert khác save thế nào và ảnh hưởng tới lifecycle.
Dùng ví dụ bulk import hoặc tạo user.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ create() insert row vào database.
Dùng save() cho mọi bulk operation.
Kỳ vọng insert() hoạt động y hệt save().

ja.title: save、insert、create
ja.displayQuestion: TypeORM の save()、insert()、create() はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: write method の意味、永続化動作、performance trade-off を確認する。
guidance: memory 上の entity 作成と database write を分けて説明させる。
insert と save の違い、lifecycle への影響を確認する。
bulk import や user 作成の例を使う。
commonMistakes: create() が database に insert すると考える。
すべての bulk operation に save() を使う。
insert() が save() と完全に同じ動作をすると期待する。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Repository
sourceReferences.note: Used for repository create, save, and insert method behavior.

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Insert Query Builder
sourceReferences.note: Used for direct insert operation context and performance-oriented insert cases.

intent: Check whether the candidate can model a one-to-many relationship in TypeORM and understands which side owns the foreign key.
primaryQuestion: How do you define a One-to-Many / Many-to-One relationship in TypeORM?

expectedSignals.label: Uses a concrete example such as User has many Posts and Post belongs to one User.

requirements.description: Uses a concrete User and Post example or equivalent parent-child domain
States that one User can have many Posts
States that each Post belongs to one User in the example

expectedSignals.label: Defines @OneToMany on the inverse collection side and @ManyToOne on the owning side.

requirements.description: Places @OneToMany on the inverse collection side such as User.posts
Places @ManyToOne on the owning side such as Post.user
Does not assume @OneToMany alone creates the foreign key column

expectedSignals.label: Explains that the many-to-one side usually owns the foreign key column in the database.

requirements.description: Identifies that the Post table stores the userId foreign key in the User/Post example
Explains that the many-to-one side owns the foreign key
Connects the foreign key column to how the relation is joined or loaded

expectedSignals.label: Mentions inverse-side callbacks such as user => user.posts and post => post.user.

requirements.description: Uses inverse-side callback such as user => user.posts
Uses inverse-side callback such as post => post.user
Explains that callbacks tell TypeORM how the two relation properties connect

expectedSignals.label: Discusses practical options such as nullable, cascade, onDelete, and explicit relation loading when relevant.

requirements.description: Considers nullable and onDelete behavior for the foreign key
Considers cascade only when child lifecycle should truly follow parent operations
Mentions explicit relation loading for endpoints that need posts rather than always loading them

scoringHints.description: Models both sides correctly, identifies the owning foreign-key side, and discusses relation options and loading behavior.
Can write the decorators but misses one detail such as FK ownership or cascade implications.
Knows relation names but cannot map them to database structure.
Answer is generic and does not show TypeORM relationship modeling ability.

followUps.question: In User and Post, which table stores the userId foreign key and why?
followUps.purpose: Verify owning-side and database mapping understanding.

followUps.question: When would you avoid cascade on this relationship?
followUps.purpose: Check practical relation option judgment.

en.title: One-to-many relationship mapping
en.displayQuestion: How do you define a One-to-Many / Many-to-One relationship in TypeORM?
en.displayIntent: Check relation decorators, inverse side, and foreign-key ownership.
guidance: Use a User/Post example.
Ask which side owns the foreign key.
Probe nullable, cascade, onDelete, and relation loading.
commonMistakes: Only defining @OneToMany.
Not knowing where the foreign key lives.
Assuming relations are always preloaded.

vi.title: Mapping quan hệ one-to-many
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn định nghĩa quan hệ One-to-Many / Many-to-One trong TypeORM như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra decorator quan hệ, inverse side và ownership của foreign key.
guidance: Dùng ví dụ User/Post.
Hỏi bên nào sở hữu foreign key.
Đào sâu nullable, cascade, onDelete và relation loading.
commonMistakes: Chỉ định nghĩa @OneToMany.
Không biết foreign key nằm ở bảng nào.
Cho rằng relation luôn được preload.

ja.title: one-to-many relation mapping
ja.displayQuestion: TypeORM で One-to-Many / Many-to-One relationship をどのように定義しますか。
ja.displayIntent: relation decorators、inverse side、foreign-key ownership を確認する。
guidance: User/Post の例を使う。
どちらが foreign key を所有するか質問する。
nullable、cascade、onDelete、relation loading を掘り下げる。
commonMistakes: @OneToMany だけを定義する。
foreign key がどの table にあるか説明できない。
relation が常に preload されると思い込む。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Many-to-one / one-to-many relations
sourceReferences.note: Used for one-to-many and many-to-one decorator patterns and relation loading examples.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can define a many-to-many relationship with TypeORM and understands join table ownership and when to promote a join table into a real entity.
primaryQuestion: What is @ManyToMany, and how do you create a join table?

expectedSignals.label: Explains @ManyToMany as a relationship where many rows on each side can relate to many rows on the other side.

requirements.description: Defines many-to-many as many rows on each side relating to many rows on the other side
Mentions that a join table represents the relationship in the database
Distinguishes many-to-many from one-to-many ownership with a single foreign key

expectedSignals.label: Uses @JoinTable on exactly one owning side to tell TypeORM to create or use a join table.

requirements.description: Places @JoinTable on exactly one owning side of the many-to-many relation
Explains that @JoinTable tells TypeORM which side owns join table metadata
Explains that putting @JoinTable on both sides is incorrect or ambiguous

expectedSignals.label: Provides a concrete example such as Question and Category or User and Role.

requirements.description: Uses a concrete example such as Question and Category
Or uses a concrete example such as User and Role
Maps the example to the join table rows connecting both sides

expectedSignals.label: Mentions optional customization of join table name and join column names.

requirements.description: Mentions customizing the join table name when naming conventions require it
Mentions customizing joinColumn and inverseJoinColumn names
Connects customization to existing database schema or naming standards

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that if the join needs extra columns such as assignedAt, role, order, or metadata, it should usually become an explicit entity with two many-to-one relations.

requirements.description: Recognizes that assignedAt, assignedBy, order, role, or metadata cannot be modeled well as a bare many-to-many join
Creates an explicit join entity such as UserRole for joins with extra columns
Models the explicit join entity with two many-to-one relations back to the original entities

scoringHints.description: Defines many-to-many with one owning @JoinTable side, explains the join table, custom naming, and when to model an explicit join entity.
Can write a basic @ManyToMany but misses custom join table or association-entity trade-offs.
Knows @ManyToMany by name but cannot explain join table structure.
Answer does not demonstrate practical many-to-many modeling.

followUps.question: If UserRole needs assignedAt and assignedBy fields, would you still use @ManyToMany? Why?
followUps.purpose: Check ability to choose explicit join entity when needed.

followUps.question: Which side gets @JoinTable, and what happens if you put it on both sides?
followUps.purpose: Verify owning-side join table understanding.

en.title: Many-to-many and join tables
en.displayQuestion: What is @ManyToMany, and how do you create a join table?
en.displayIntent: Check many-to-many mapping, join table ownership, and association-entity trade-offs.
guidance: Use a concrete User/Role or Question/Category example.
Ask where @JoinTable is placed.
Probe when extra association fields require a separate entity.
commonMistakes: Putting @JoinTable on both sides.
Not knowing the physical join table.
Using @ManyToMany when the relationship has its own data.

vi.title: Many-to-many và join table
vi.displayQuestion: @ManyToMany là gì, và bạn tạo join table như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra mapping many-to-many, ownership của join table và trade-off khi cần entity trung gian.
guidance: Dùng ví dụ User/Role hoặc Question/Category.
Hỏi @JoinTable đặt ở đâu.
Đào sâu trường hợp association có field riêng và cần entity riêng.
commonMistakes: Đặt @JoinTable ở cả hai bên.
Không biết join table vật lý là gì.
Dùng @ManyToMany khi relationship có dữ liệu riêng.

ja.title: many-to-many と join table
ja.displayQuestion: @ManyToMany とは何で、join table をどのように作りますか。
ja.displayIntent: many-to-many mapping、join table ownership、association entity の trade-off を確認する。
guidance: User/Role または Question/Category の例を使う。
@JoinTable をどちらに置くか質問する。
association に独自 field がある場合に separate entity が必要か掘り下げる。
commonMistakes: @JoinTable を両側に置く。
物理的な join table を説明できない。
relationship 自体にデータがあるのに @ManyToMany を使う。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Many-to-many relations
sourceReferences.note: Used for @ManyToMany, @JoinTable, and explicit join entity guidance.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands migrations as version-controlled database schema changes and can describe safe generation and execution in TypeORM.
primaryQuestion: What is a database migration, and how do you generate/run one in TypeORM?

expectedSignals.label: Explains a migration as a versioned, reviewable schema/data change with up and down methods.

requirements.description: Defines a migration as a versioned database schema or data change
Mentions up and down methods for applying and reverting the change
Treats migrations as reviewable code rather than runtime magic

expectedSignals.label: Mentions using a configured DataSource file with TypeORM CLI commands such as migration:generate and migration:run.

requirements.description: Mentions using the configured TypeORM DataSource file for CLI commands
Mentions migration:generate for generating a diff from entity metadata
Mentions migration:run for applying pending migrations

expectedSignals.label: Understands generated migrations should be reviewed before committing or running.

requirements.description: Reviews generated SQL or operations before merging
Checks for unintended drops, renames interpreted as drop/add, or data-loss operations
Checks lock, rewrite, index build, and runtime impact on large production tables

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes generating from current entity diff versus manually creating a migration.

requirements.description: Explains that generated migrations come from comparing entity metadata with the database/schema state
Explains that manual migrations are written for changes the generator cannot infer safely
Chooses manual migration for data backfills, multi-step changes, or production-safe DDL sequencing

expectedSignals.label: Mentions production concerns such as backups, locks, rollback plan, and avoiding synchronize in production.

requirements.description: Plans backup, rollback, and verification before production migration
Considers table locks, long-running DDL, index builds, and deployment sequencing
Avoids using synchronize as the production schema-change mechanism

scoringHints.description: Explains migration purpose, TypeORM CLI flow, DataSource usage, review discipline, production safety, and rollback planning.
Understands basic generate/run flow but misses production safety or revert details.
Has run a command before but cannot explain what it does or how to validate it.
Answer does not show reliable migration understanding.

followUps.question: What exactly would you review in a generated migration before merging it?
followUps.purpose: Check migration review discipline.

followUps.question: Why might a generated migration be unsafe on a large production table?
followUps.purpose: Probe production schema-change risk awareness.

en.title: TypeORM migrations
en.displayQuestion: What is a database migration, and how do you generate/run one in TypeORM?
en.displayIntent: Check migration purpose, CLI workflow, and production schema-change safety.
guidance: Ask for generate, run, and revert flow.
Confirm DataSource file usage and migration review.
Probe why migrations replace synchronize in production.
commonMistakes: Using synchronize in production.
Committing generated SQL without review.
Not planning rollback or lock impact.

vi.title: Migration trong TypeORM
vi.displayQuestion: Database migration là gì, và bạn generate/run migration trong TypeORM như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra mục đích migration, CLI workflow và an toàn khi đổi schema production.
guidance: Hỏi flow generate, run và revert.
Xác nhận cách dùng DataSource file và review migration.
Đào sâu lý do migration thay cho synchronize ở production.
commonMistakes: Dùng synchronize ở production.
Commit SQL generate mà không review.
Không có kế hoạch rollback hoặc đánh giá lock impact.

ja.title: TypeORM migration
ja.displayQuestion: database migration とは何で、TypeORM でどのように generate/run しますか。
ja.displayIntent: migration の目的、CLI workflow、本番 schema change の安全性を確認する。
guidance: generate、run、revert の流れを質問する。
DataSource file の使用と migration review を確認する。
本番で synchronize の代わりに migration を使う理由を掘り下げる。
commonMistakes: 本番で synchronize を使う。
生成された SQL を review せず commit する。
rollback や lock impact を考えない。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Migrations
sourceReferences.note: Used for migration generation, execution, DataSource option, and transaction behavior.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands transaction atomicity and the correct TypeORM APIs for running multiple database operations as one unit of work.
primaryQuestion: What is a Transaction, and how do you use it in TypeORM?

expectedSignals.label: Explains a transaction as a set of operations that commit together or roll back together.

requirements.description: Defines a transaction as a group of database operations that commit together
Mentions rollback if one operation fails
Connects transactions to preserving consistency across related database writes

expectedSignals.label: Uses DataSource.transaction or EntityManager.transaction for common transaction scopes.

requirements.description: Uses DataSource.transaction for common scoped transaction work
Mentions EntityManager.transaction or equivalent TypeORM transaction helper
Keeps the transaction scope limited to the database work that must be atomic

expectedSignals.label: States that all operations inside the transaction must use the provided transactional manager or repositories from it.

requirements.description: States that all queries inside the callback must use the provided transactional manager
Uses repositories obtained from the transactional manager when repository APIs are needed
Explains that using this.userRepository inside the transaction may run outside the transaction scope

expectedSignals.label: Mentions QueryRunner for lower-level manual transaction control when needed.

requirements.description: Mentions QueryRunner for manual transaction control
Mentions explicit connect, startTransaction, commit, rollback, and release lifecycle when using QueryRunner
Uses QueryRunner when lower-level connection or transaction control is required

expectedSignals.label: Connects transactions to real workflows such as creating an order and updating inventory or wallet balances.

requirements.description: Applies a transaction to a workflow such as creating an order and updating inventory
Avoids holding the database transaction open around a payment provider or other external API call
Explains the consistency and latency trade-off around external side effects and database transactions

scoringHints.description: Explains atomicity, correct transactional manager usage, rollback behavior, QueryRunner use cases, and practical consistency boundaries.
Understands transaction basics but misses manager scoping or external side-effect risks.
Knows transactions are about safety but cannot use TypeORM APIs correctly.
Answer lacks enough detail to trust transactional correctness.

followUps.question: What goes wrong if you use this.userRepository inside dataSource.transaction instead of the transactional manager?
followUps.purpose: Verify transaction scoping knowledge.

followUps.question: Would you call a payment provider inside the database transaction? Why or why not?
followUps.purpose: Check consistency versus side-effect trade-offs.

en.title: Transactions in TypeORM
en.displayQuestion: What is a Transaction, and how do you use it in TypeORM?
en.displayIntent: Check atomicity, transactional manager usage, and consistency boundaries.
guidance: Use a multi-step business workflow example.
Confirm all queries use the transactional manager.
Discuss QueryRunner and external side-effect risks.
commonMistakes: Using global repositories inside a transaction.
Opening long transactions around external API calls.
Ignoring rollback and isolation behavior.

vi.title: Transaction trong TypeORM
vi.displayQuestion: Transaction là gì, và bạn dùng transaction trong TypeORM như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra atomicity, cách dùng transactional manager và ranh giới consistency.
guidance: Dùng ví dụ business workflow nhiều bước.
Xác nhận mọi query dùng transactional manager.
Nói về QueryRunner và rủi ro khi gọi external side effect.
commonMistakes: Dùng global repository bên trong transaction.
Mở transaction dài quanh external API call.
Bỏ qua rollback và isolation behavior.

ja.title: TypeORM の transaction
ja.displayQuestion: Transaction とは何で、TypeORM でどのように使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: atomicity、transactional manager の使い方、consistency boundary を確認する。
guidance: 複数 step の business workflow 例を使う。
すべての query が transactional manager を使うか確認する。
QueryRunner と external side effect のリスクを話す。
commonMistakes: transaction 内で global repository を使う。
external API call を含めて長い transaction を開く。
rollback と isolation behavior を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Transactions
sourceReferences.note: Used for DataSource.transaction, transactional EntityManager, isolation levels, and QueryRunner context.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands eager and lazy relation loading, the convenience/performance trade-offs, and how to avoid hidden N+1 queries or over-fetching.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between eager and lazy loading of relations?

expectedSignals.label: Explains eager loading as automatically loading a relation when using repository find methods if configured on the relation.

requirements.description: Defines eager loading as automatic relation loading when configured on a relation
Mentions that eager relations apply to repository find methods
Warns that eager loading can over-fetch on high-traffic list endpoints

expectedSignals.label: Explains lazy loading as deferring relation access until a Promise-backed relation property is awaited.

requirements.description: Defines lazy loading as deferring relation query until the relation property is accessed
Mentions Promise-backed relation properties
Warns about hidden queries, N+1 behavior, or unexpected async access with lazy relations

expectedSignals.label: Mentions explicit relation loading through find options or QueryBuilder joins as a common alternative.

requirements.description: Mentions loading relations explicitly through find options
Mentions QueryBuilder joins for explicit relation loading
Applies explicit loading to load posts for a user only on endpoints that need them

expectedSignals.label: Discusses over-fetching risk with eager relations and hidden N+1 or unexpected queries with lazy relations.

requirements.description: Explains eager relation over-fetching and payload/query cost
Explains hidden N+1 or unexpected query risk with lazy relations
Chooses relation loading strategy per endpoint or query shape

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that QueryBuilder does not automatically apply eager loading and requires explicit joins.

requirements.description: States that QueryBuilder does not automatically apply eager relations
Uses explicit leftJoinAndSelect or equivalent QueryBuilder joins
Accounts for relations being missing unless explicitly joined in QueryBuilder queries

scoringHints.description: Compares eager, lazy, and explicit loading with performance, query-shape, N+1, and QueryBuilder caveats.
Understands eager versus lazy basics but misses one practical performance or QueryBuilder detail.
Knows the terms but cannot reason about generated queries or performance.
Answer does not show safe relation-loading judgment.

followUps.question: Why might eager loading a relation on User be harmful for a high-traffic list endpoint?
followUps.purpose: Probe over-fetching and response-shape judgment.

followUps.question: How would you explicitly load posts for a user only on endpoints that need them?
followUps.purpose: Check concrete relation loading technique.

en.title: Eager versus lazy relations
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between eager and lazy loading of relations?
en.displayIntent: Check relation-loading behavior and performance trade-offs.
guidance: Compare automatic eager loading, Promise-backed lazy loading, and explicit joins.
Ask about N+1 and over-fetching.
Confirm QueryBuilder behavior.
commonMistakes: Making every relation eager.
Forgetting lazy relations are asynchronous.
Ignoring hidden query count.

vi.title: Eager và lazy relation
vi.displayQuestion: Eager loading và lazy loading của relations khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hành vi load relation và trade-off performance.
guidance: So sánh eager tự động, lazy dùng Promise và explicit join.
Hỏi về N+1 và over-fetching.
Xác nhận behavior khi dùng QueryBuilder.
commonMistakes: Đặt mọi relation thành eager.
Quên lazy relation là async.
Bỏ qua số lượng query ẩn.

ja.title: eager relation と lazy relation
ja.displayQuestion: relation の eager loading と lazy loading はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: relation loading の動作と performance trade-off を確認する。
guidance: automatic eager loading、Promise-backed lazy loading、explicit joins を比較する。
N+1 と over-fetching を質問する。
QueryBuilder の挙動を確認する。
commonMistakes: すべての relation を eager にする。
lazy relation が async であることを忘れる。
隠れた query count を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Eager and Lazy Relations
sourceReferences.note: Used for eager relation behavior, lazy promise-based relations, and QueryBuilder caveat.

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Find Options
sourceReferences.note: Used for explicit relation loading through find options.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate knows what QueryBuilder is and can choose it over repository methods when query complexity, joins, aggregations, or precise SQL control require it.
primaryQuestion: What is QueryBuilder, and when do you prefer it over Repository methods?

expectedSignals.label: Explains QueryBuilder as TypeORM's fluent API for building SQL queries with aliases, joins, where clauses, ordering, pagination, and parameters.

requirements.description: Defines QueryBuilder as TypeORM's fluent API for building SQL queries
Mentions aliases, joins, where clauses, ordering, pagination, or parameters
Gives a concrete case where find options become awkward and QueryBuilder is clearer

expectedSignals.label: Prefers simple repository methods for straightforward CRUD and simple filters.

requirements.description: Uses repository methods for straightforward CRUD and simple lookups
Avoids using QueryBuilder for every simple query without need
Chooses repository or QueryBuilder based on query complexity and readability

expectedSignals.label: Uses QueryBuilder for complex joins, conditional filters, aggregations, subqueries, locking, partial selects, or performance-sensitive query shape.

requirements.description: Uses QueryBuilder for complex joins and conditional filters
Uses QueryBuilder for aggregations, subqueries, or locking when needed
Uses QueryBuilder to control selected columns and SQL shape for performance-sensitive queries

expectedSignals.label: Mentions parameter binding to avoid SQL injection rather than string interpolation.

requirements.description: Uses named or positional parameters in QueryBuilder where clauses
Avoids interpolating user-provided search terms directly into SQL strings
Safely passes user search terms for LIKE or ILIKE clauses with bound parameters and escaping where needed

expectedSignals.label: Understands that QueryBuilder can return entities or raw results depending on getMany, getOne, getRawMany, or getRawOne.

requirements.description: Mentions getMany or getOne for entity results
Mentions getRawMany or getRawOne for raw selected results
Chooses result method based on whether the query selects full entities or raw projections/aggregates

scoringHints.description: Balances repository simplicity with QueryBuilder control, covers joins/aggregations/subqueries, safe parameters, and return shapes.
Knows QueryBuilder is for complex queries but misses security or return-shape detail.
Can call createQueryBuilder but cannot justify when or how to use it safely.
Answer lacks concrete TypeORM query-building knowledge.

followUps.question: Give an example where find() becomes awkward and QueryBuilder is clearer.
followUps.purpose: Check practical method selection.

followUps.question: How do you safely pass a user-provided search term into a QueryBuilder where clause?
followUps.purpose: Probe parameter binding and injection safety.

en.title: QueryBuilder use cases
en.displayQuestion: What is QueryBuilder, and when do you prefer it over Repository methods?
en.displayIntent: Check query-complexity judgment and safe dynamic SQL construction.
guidance: Ask for a concrete complex query example.
Compare repository methods with QueryBuilder.
Listen for parameter binding and raw versus entity results.
commonMistakes: String-interpolating user input.
Using QueryBuilder for every simple CRUD query.
Confusing raw rows with hydrated entities.

vi.title: Khi nào dùng QueryBuilder
vi.displayQuestion: QueryBuilder là gì, và khi nào bạn ưu tiên nó hơn Repository methods?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra judgment về độ phức tạp query và cách dựng SQL động an toàn.
guidance: Yêu cầu ví dụ query phức tạp cụ thể.
So sánh repository methods với QueryBuilder.
Tìm ý về parameter binding và raw result khác entity result.
commonMistakes: Nối chuỗi input của user vào SQL.
Dùng QueryBuilder cho mọi CRUD đơn giản.
Nhầm raw rows với hydrated entities.

ja.title: QueryBuilder の使いどころ
ja.displayQuestion: QueryBuilder とは何で、Repository methods よりいつ優先して使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: query complexity の判断と安全な dynamic SQL construction を確認する。
guidance: 具体的な complex query の例を求める。
repository methods と QueryBuilder を比較する。
parameter binding と raw/entity result の違いを確認する。
commonMistakes: user input を文字列連結で SQL に入れる。
簡単な CRUD すべてに QueryBuilder を使う。
raw rows と hydrated entities を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Select Query Builder
sourceReferences.note: Used for QueryBuilder aliases, parameters, joins, and result methods.

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Find Options
sourceReferences.note: Used for repository find options comparison.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands TypeOrmModule.forFeature as module-level repository provider registration in NestJS and why each feature module declares the entities it needs.
primaryQuestion: What is TypeOrmModule.forFeature(), and why is it needed per module?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that forFeature registers repository providers for selected entities in the current Nest module context.

requirements.description: Explains that TypeOrmModule.forFeature registers repositories for selected entities
Mentions that registration applies to the current Nest module context
Connects forFeature to repository providers Nest can inject

expectedSignals.label: Connects forFeature([User]) with @InjectRepository(User) being resolvable in providers of that module.

requirements.description: States that the module containing UsersService must import TypeOrmModule.forFeature([User])
Connects forFeature([User]) to @InjectRepository(User) resolution
Checks provider/module scope when repository injection fails

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes forRoot or forRootAsync connection setup from forFeature entity/repository registration.

requirements.description: Explains forRoot or forRootAsync as database connection/DataSource setup
Explains forFeature as entity/repository registration for a feature module
Avoids reconfiguring the database connection in every feature module

expectedSignals.label: Mentions importing another module that exports a service instead of directly reaching across module boundaries.

requirements.description: Uses module exports/imports to share services across module boundaries
Avoids directly reaching into another module's repository without proper module ownership
Keeps cross-feature operations behind exported services when appropriate

expectedSignals.label: Understands that multi-database or named DataSource setups may require passing the data source name consistently.

requirements.description: Mentions named DataSource or multi-database setups
Passes the correct data source name to forFeature when required
Passes the same data source name to @InjectRepository when required

scoringHints.description: Explains provider registration, module visibility, forRoot versus forFeature, repository tokens, and feature-module boundaries.
Knows the syntax but misses one detail such as provider visibility or named data sources.
Has seen forFeature but cannot explain why dependency resolution fails without it.
Answer does not show NestJS TypeORM module wiring knowledge.

followUps.question: UsersService injects Repository<User>. Which module must import TypeOrmModule.forFeature([User])?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete module ownership reasoning.

followUps.question: How is TypeOrmModule.forRoot different from TypeOrmModule.forFeature?
followUps.purpose: Verify connection setup versus repository registration distinction.

en.title: TypeOrmModule.forFeature
en.displayQuestion: What is TypeOrmModule.forFeature(), and why is it needed per module?
en.displayIntent: Check NestJS module-level repository provider registration.
guidance: Ask how @InjectRepository is resolved.
Compare forRoot and forFeature.
Probe module boundaries and provider visibility.
commonMistakes: Assuming repositories are globally visible.
Putting all entities in AppModule by habit.
Making modules global to hide wiring mistakes.

vi.title: TypeOrmModule.forFeature
vi.displayQuestion: TypeOrmModule.forFeature() là gì, và vì sao mỗi module cần khai báo riêng?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cách đăng ký repository provider theo module trong NestJS.
guidance: Hỏi @InjectRepository được resolve như thế nào.
So sánh forRoot và forFeature.
Đào sâu module boundary và provider visibility.
commonMistakes: Cho rằng repository nào cũng visible toàn app.
Nhét mọi entity vào AppModule theo thói quen.
Biến module thành global để che lỗi wiring.

ja.title: TypeOrmModule.forFeature
ja.displayQuestion: TypeOrmModule.forFeature() とは何で、なぜ module ごとに必要ですか。
ja.displayIntent: NestJS module-level repository provider registration を確認する。
guidance: @InjectRepository がどのように resolve されるか質問する。
forRoot と forFeature を比較する。
module boundary と provider visibility を掘り下げる。
commonMistakes: repository が app 全体で global に見えると思い込む。
習慣で全 entity を AppModule に入れる。
wiring mistake を隠すために module を global にする。

sourceReferences.note: Used for TypeOrmModule.forFeature and @InjectRepository repository injection.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can separate API contracts from persistence models and avoid leaking database implementation details or sensitive fields.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between a DTO and an Entity?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that an Entity models database persistence with TypeORM decorators and table/column metadata.

requirements.description: Explains that an entity models database persistence
Mentions TypeORM decorators such as @Entity and @Column
Mentions table and column metadata as part of entity definition

expectedSignals.label: Explains that a DTO models data crossing an application boundary such as request body, response body, or service command.

requirements.description: Explains that DTOs model data crossing API or application boundaries
Distinguishes request DTOs, response DTOs, and service command shapes where relevant
Allows DTO shape to differ from database table shape

expectedSignals.label: Mentions DTOs often carry validation, transformation, and API documentation concerns.

requirements.description: Mentions validation concerns on DTOs
Mentions transformation or serialization concerns on DTOs
Mentions API documentation/schema concerns when relevant

expectedSignals.label: Explains why returning raw entities can leak internal fields such as passwordHash, soft-delete markers, or internal status columns.

requirements.description: Identifies sensitive fields such as passwordHash or tokens that must not be exposed
Identifies internal fields such as deletedAt, internal status, audit flags, or version columns
Maps entities to response DTOs to avoid leaking persistence details

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes mapping between DTOs and entities as intentional boundary code, not unnecessary duplication in many real systems.

requirements.description: Explains why DTO/entity mapping can be intentional boundary code
Explains when reusing a class shape is acceptable for small or internal cases
Explains when reuse is too risky because persistence fields, validation, or public contracts diverge

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates persistence, request validation, response shaping, security, and mapping responsibilities with concrete examples.
Understands the basic distinction but misses security or response-contract implications.
Can define DTO and Entity but does not know why separation matters.
Answer risks conflating API and persistence boundaries.

followUps.question: Which UserEntity fields would you never expose in a UserResponseDto?
followUps.purpose: Check data exposure and API contract reasoning.

followUps.question: When is it acceptable to reuse a class shape, and when is the risk too high?
followUps.purpose: Assess pragmatic boundary judgment rather than dogma.

en.title: DTO versus Entity
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between a DTO and an Entity?
en.displayIntent: Check API boundary and persistence model separation.
guidance: Ask for request DTO, response DTO, and entity examples.
Listen for validation, persistence metadata, and sensitive field exposure.
Probe mapping trade-offs.
commonMistakes: Returning raw entities from public APIs.
Putting request-only fields on entities.
Treating DTOs as pointless duplication.

vi.title: DTO khác Entity
vi.displayQuestion: DTO và Entity khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ranh giới giữa API contract và persistence model.
guidance: Hỏi ví dụ request DTO, response DTO và entity.
Tìm ý về validation, persistence metadata và lộ field nhạy cảm.
Đào sâu trade-off khi mapping.
commonMistakes: Trả raw entity qua public API.
Đưa field chỉ thuộc request vào entity.
Xem DTO là duplication vô ích.

ja.title: DTO と Entity の違い
ja.displayQuestion: DTO と Entity はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: API boundary と persistence model の分離を確認する。
guidance: request DTO、response DTO、entity の例を求める。
validation、persistence metadata、sensitive field exposure を確認する。
mapping の trade-off を掘り下げる。
commonMistakes: public API で raw entity を返す。
request-only field を entity に置く。
DTO を無意味な duplication と考える。

intent: Check whether the candidate understands TypeORM soft delete mechanics, query behavior, restoration, and the business/data-retention implications of keeping deleted rows.
primaryQuestion: How do you implement soft delete in TypeORM?

expectedSignals.label: Mentions adding @DeleteDateColumn or a deletedAt column to the entity.

requirements.description: Mentions @DeleteDateColumn for TypeORM-managed soft delete timestamp
Mentions a deletedAt column as the persistence marker
Explains that setting the delete timestamp marks the row as deleted without removing it

expectedSignals.label: Uses repository.softDelete, softRemove, restore, or recover depending on whether working by criteria or entity instance.

requirements.description: Uses softDelete for criteria-based soft deletion
Uses softRemove when working with entity instances
Mentions restore or recover for undoing soft delete depending on criteria versus entity instance

expectedSignals.label: Explains that soft-deleted rows remain in the database and are normally excluded from standard find results.

requirements.description: States that soft-deleted rows remain physically present in the database
States that standard find results normally exclude soft-deleted rows
Connects retaining rows to audit, restore, or historical needs

expectedSignals.label: Mentions withDeleted when intentionally querying soft-deleted rows.

requirements.description: Mentions withDeleted when querying soft-deleted rows intentionally
Applies withDeleted to a case such as an admin audit screen
Avoids exposing soft-deleted rows in normal user-facing queries unintentionally

expectedSignals.label: Discusses practical implications such as unique constraints, audit/history, restore behavior, and when hard delete is still required.

requirements.description: Explains how a unique email constraint can still block a new user after the old row is soft-deleted
Considers restore behavior and conflicts with newer records
Recognizes cases where hard delete, anonymization, or retention policy is still required

scoringHints.description: Explains DeleteDateColumn, softDelete/softRemove/restore/recover, default query filtering, withDeleted, and business constraints.
Knows DeleteDateColumn and softDelete but misses restore, withDeleted, or unique-constraint implications.
Knows the term soft delete but cannot implement it reliably in TypeORM.
Answer lacks enough detail for production use of soft delete.

followUps.question: What problem can a unique email constraint cause after a user is soft-deleted?
followUps.purpose: Check database constraint and retention reasoning.

followUps.question: How would you query including soft-deleted rows for an admin audit screen?
followUps.purpose: Verify withDeleted and intentional query behavior.

en.title: Soft delete in TypeORM
en.displayQuestion: How do you implement soft delete in TypeORM?
en.displayIntent: Check soft-delete mechanics, query behavior, and data-retention trade-offs.
guidance: Ask for DeleteDateColumn and repository methods.
Probe default filtering and withDeleted.
Discuss unique constraints, restore, and hard-delete requirements.
commonMistakes: Thinking soft delete removes the row.
Ignoring unique constraint conflicts.
Assuming soft delete satisfies privacy deletion requirements.

guidance: Hỏi về DeleteDateColumn và repository methods.
Đào sâu default filtering và withDeleted.
Nói về unique constraint, restore và yêu cầu hard delete.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ soft delete xoá row khỏi database.
Bỏ qua conflict với unique constraint.
Cho rằng soft delete luôn đáp ứng yêu cầu xoá dữ liệu riêng tư.

ja.title: TypeORM の soft delete

guidance: DeleteDateColumn と repository methods を質問する。
default filtering と withDeleted を掘り下げる。
unique constraint、restore、hard delete requirement を話す。
commonMistakes: soft delete が row を物理削除すると考える。

intent: Check whether the candidate understands common TypeORM column decorators and the database metadata they create.

expectedSignals.label: Explains @Column as mapping a property to a database column with options such as type, nullable, length, default, unique, precision, and select.

requirements.description: Explains that @Column maps an entity property to a database column
Mentions options such as type, nullable, length, default, unique, precision, or select
Can define a unique email column in the example

expectedSignals.label: Explains @PrimaryGeneratedColumn as a generated primary key column, commonly increment or uuid depending on configuration.

requirements.description: Explains @PrimaryGeneratedColumn as a generated primary key column
Mentions increment or uuid generation strategies
Can define a UUID primary key when asked

expectedSignals.label: Explains @CreateDateColumn as automatically set when the entity is inserted.

requirements.description: Explains that @CreateDateColumn stores the creation timestamp
Mentions it is set automatically when the entity is inserted
Distinguishes automatic create timestamp from manually assigned date fields

expectedSignals.label: Explains @UpdateDateColumn as automatically updated when the entity is updated through TypeORM persistence operations.

requirements.description: Explains that @UpdateDateColumn stores the last update timestamp
Mentions it is updated through TypeORM persistence operations

expectedSignals.label: Mentions choosing explicit database types and options for important fields instead of relying blindly on defaults.

requirements.description: Sets explicit database type when default inference is not precise enough
Sets precision, nullable, length, or default intentionally for important fields
Avoids relying blindly on TypeORM defaults for money, enums, timestamps, or constrained fields

scoringHints.description: Explains each decorator, common options, generated key strategies, automatic timestamps, and schema-design implications.

Can list decorators but cannot connect them to database columns.
Answer does not show enough TypeORM decorator knowledge.

followUps.question: When would you explicitly set column type, precision, or nullable instead of relying on defaults?
followUps.purpose: Check schema-design judgment.

en.title: Common column decorators

guidance: Ask for each decorator's purpose.
Probe common column options and primary key strategy.
Discuss automatic timestamp behavior.
commonMistakes: Treating decorators as TypeScript-only.
Not knowing generated primary key strategies.
Ignoring column type and nullability options.

vi.title: Các column decorator phổ biến

guidance: Hỏi mục đích của từng decorator.
Đào sâu option column phổ biến và chiến lược primary key.
Nói về hành vi automatic timestamp.
commonMistakes: Xem decorator chỉ là TypeScript-only.
Không biết các strategy primary key generated.
Bỏ qua option column type và nullability.

ja.title: よく使う column decorator

guidance: 各 decorator の目的を質問する。
一般的な column options と primary key strategy を掘り下げる。
automatic timestamp の動作を話す。
commonMistakes: decorator を TypeScript-only と考える。
generated primary key strategies を知らない。
column type と nullability options を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: TypeORM docs: Entities
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Column, @PrimaryGeneratedColumn, @CreateDateColumn, and @UpdateDateColumn behavior.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands synchronize: true, why it is convenient for local development, and why it is dangerous for production schema management.
primaryQuestion: What does synchronize: true do in TypeORM config, and why is it dangerous in production?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that synchronize makes TypeORM automatically update the database schema to match entity metadata on application startup.

requirements.description: Explains that synchronize updates the database schema to match entity metadata
Mentions that this happens on application startup
Explains that renaming an entity property can trigger schema changes automatically

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes it can be convenient for local experiments or prototypes but should not be the production schema-change mechanism.

requirements.description: Recognizes synchronize can be convenient for local development, experiments, or throwaway prototypes
States it should not be the production schema-change mechanism
Distinguishes local convenience from production safety requirements

expectedSignals.label: Mentions risks such as unintended column/table changes, data loss, unreviewed DDL, startup-time schema changes, and environment drift.

requirements.description: Mentions data loss or unintended drops/additions as a synchronize risk
Mentions unreviewed DDL running at application startup
Mentions environment drift or startup behavior differing between environments

expectedSignals.label: Recommends explicit migrations for production with review, testing, backup, rollout, and rollback planning.

requirements.description: Recommends explicit migrations for production schema changes
Requires review, testing, backup, and verification before production migration
Plans rollout and rollback rather than letting app startup mutate schema

expectedSignals.label: Understands that production schema changes need operational awareness such as locks, long-running migrations, and deployment sequencing.

requirements.description: Considers locks, index builds, table rewrites, or long-running migration impact
Considers application deployment order and backward-compatible schema changes
Considers special strategy for large tables such as phased nullable columns, backfills, or online index creation

scoringHints.description: Explains startup schema synchronization, appropriate local use, concrete production risks, and migration-based operational discipline.
Knows synchronize should be off in production but misses some risk details or migration process.
Has heard it is dangerous but cannot explain why.
Answer suggests unsafe production schema management.

followUps.question: What could happen if you rename an entity property while synchronize is enabled against production?
followUps.purpose: Check concrete data-loss and schema-drift reasoning.

followUps.question: When, if ever, would you allow synchronize: true?
followUps.purpose: Assess pragmatic environment-specific judgment.

en.title: synchronize true risk
en.displayQuestion: What does synchronize: true do in TypeORM config, and why is it dangerous in production?
en.displayIntent: Check schema synchronization behavior and production migration judgment.
guidance: Ask what happens at application startup.
Probe concrete production risks.
Compare with reviewed migrations and rollback planning.
commonMistakes: Using synchronize as production migration strategy.
Assuming schema sync is always safe.
Ignoring data loss and lock risks.

vi.title: Rủi ro synchronize true
vi.displayQuestion: synchronize: true trong TypeORM config làm gì, và vì sao nó nguy hiểm ở production?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hành vi schema synchronization và judgment về migration production.
guidance: Hỏi điều gì xảy ra khi app startup.
Đào sâu rủi ro production cụ thể.
So sánh với migration được review và rollback plan.
commonMistakes: Dùng synchronize làm chiến lược migration production.
Cho rằng schema sync luôn an toàn.
Bỏ qua rủi ro mất dữ liệu và lock.

ja.title: synchronize true のリスク
ja.displayQuestion: TypeORM config の synchronize: true は何を行い、なぜ production では危険ですか。
ja.displayIntent: schema synchronization の動作と production migration judgment を確認する。
guidance: application startup 時に何が起きるか質問する。
具体的な production risk を掘り下げる。
reviewed migration と rollback planning と比較する。
commonMistakes: synchronize を production migration strategy として使う。
schema sync が常に安全だと思い込む。
data loss と lock risk を無視する。
